question: What is dependency injection and why use it on Android?
answer:
Dependency injection (DI) means giving an object its collaborators from outside, rather than letting it construct or look them up itself.

Why on Android:
• Testability — swap real services for fakes in unit tests.
• Explicit dependencies — class signature shows everything it needs.
• Lifetime control — singleton vs per-screen vs per-feature scope is centralised.
• Avoids the service-locator anti-pattern (`SomeService.getInstance()`) embedded in business code.

Approaches:
• Manual DI — pure constructor injection. Works fine for small apps.
• Hilt — Google's recommended DI framework, built on Dagger 2. Annotation-driven, integrates with Android lifecycles (@HiltViewModel, @AndroidEntryPoint).
• Dagger 2 — full power, more boilerplate. Used in large enterprise apps.
• Koin — service-locator-flavored, runtime DSL; no codegen, slower at runtime, easier setup.
• Kodein, Anvil — niche choices.

question: What is Hilt? How does it differ from Dagger 2?
answer:
Hilt is Google's official DI library for Android. It's Dagger 2 with opinionated defaults:

• Annotation-driven entry points: `@HiltAndroidApp` on Application, `@AndroidEntryPoint` on Activity/Fragment/Service, `@HiltViewModel` on ViewModels.
• Predefined component scopes mapped to Android lifecycles: SingletonComponent, ActivityComponent, ViewModelComponent, FragmentComponent.
• `@InstallIn(...)` declares which component a module belongs to.
• Less boilerplate than vanilla Dagger; you don't write `Component`/`Subcomponent` interfaces.

Dagger 2 still works underneath; if you need full control (custom subcomponents, multibinding for plugins), you fall back to Dagger features. Most apps don't need that.

Migration: starting greenfield → use Hilt. Big legacy Dagger codebase → migrate gradually.

question: What are scopes in Hilt and how do they map to component lifecycles?
answer:
Hilt components are tied to Android lifecycles, and scopes attach a binding to a particular component's lifetime:

• `@Singleton` → SingletonComponent — one instance per Application.
• `@ActivityRetainedScoped` → ActivityRetainedComponent — survives configuration changes (rotation). Same lifetime as ViewModelStoreOwner.
• `@ActivityScoped` → ActivityComponent — one per Activity instance (re-created on rotation by default).
• `@FragmentScoped` → FragmentComponent.
• `@ViewModelScoped` → ViewModelComponent — bindings shared by collaborators of a single @HiltViewModel.
• `@ServiceScoped` → ServiceComponent.

Unscoped bindings are recreated every injection — useful for stateless helpers; expensive to recreate types should be scoped.

Gotcha: a scope is only useful if there's shared state to preserve. Don't scope something that's already a Singleton.

question: What are @Qualifier and multibinding in Dagger/Hilt?
answer:
Qualifier — distinguishes multiple bindings of the same type. You annotate the binding and the injection point with the same qualifier; Dagger picks based on it.

Use cases:
• Multiple OkHttpClient instances (auth vs non-auth).
• Different Dispatchers (`@IoDispatcher`, `@MainDispatcher`, `@DefaultDispatcher`).
• Multiple Retrofit instances against different base URLs.

Multibinding — bind multiple values into a single Set or Map. Useful for plugin-like architectures.
• `@IntoSet` — adds the binding to `Set<T>`.
• `@IntoMap` + `@StringKey("...")` / `@ClassKey(...)` — adds to a Map.

Multibindings unlock feature-module composition: each feature module contributes its routes / settings entries; the app composes them all.

question: How do you replace dependencies in tests with Hilt?
answer:
Hilt provides the `@HiltAndroidTest` rule and `@TestInstallIn` / `@UninstallModules` to swap real bindings for fakes in instrumented tests.

For unit tests, prefer NOT to involve Hilt at all — instantiate ViewModels and Repositories with fakes directly; that's faster and simpler.

For instrumented (UI) tests:
• Annotate the test class with `@HiltAndroidTest`.
• Use `HiltAndroidRule` to inject members.
• Use `@UninstallModules(NetworkModule::class)` to remove the real module + provide a fake module annotated `@InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class)` in the test source set.
• Or use `@TestInstallIn(components = [...], replaces = [...])` for cleaner replacement.

For Compose UI tests, combine the Hilt rule with `createAndroidComposeRule<HiltComponentActivity>()`.

question: Hilt vs Koin — when to choose which?
answer:
Both are common Android DI choices.

Hilt:
• Compile-time codegen (KSP/KAPT) — errors at build time, no runtime surprise.
• Better build performance for very large graphs.
• Tight integration with Jetpack (ViewModel, Compose, Navigation).
• Steeper learning curve, more annotations.

Koin:
• Pure Kotlin DSL, no codegen — easier setup.
• Lazy / runtime resolution — slightly slower at first access; missing bindings fail at runtime.
• Lighter initial mental load.
• Less ergonomic for complex graphs (subcomponents, qualifiers across modules).

Guideline:
• Greenfield Android-only app, single team → either; Hilt is the official path.
• KMP shared module → Koin (it's multiplatform-ready; Hilt is Android-only).
• Big enterprise codebase, many feature modules → Hilt for stronger guarantees.
• Prototype, learning project → Koin for the lower barrier.
